title: Subscription Service Cascade Attack via Stolen Card Credentials
narrative: Fraudster exploits a compromised card by rapidly enrolling in 8-15 low-friction subscription services ($9.99-$29.99 each) within a 45-90 minute window, typically between 2am-5am cardholder local time. Attacker targets services with instant digital activation and no AVS enforcementâ€”streaming platforms, cloud storage, VPN providers, meal kit trials. Transactions cluster at $14.99 and $19.99 price points. The card shows first-time use with each merchant (card1_MID_count=1 for all), ProductCD='W' (web-initiated), and identical P_emaildomain across all subscriptions but mismatched to card's historical email pattern. The dist1 values (distance between billing and IP geolocation) exceed 800km consistently. Card velocity jumps from 1-2 monthly subscriptions to 12+ in under an hour. Fraudster monetizes by reselling accounts on dark web forums within 6-12 hours before chargebacks materialize.
indicators: 8-15 transactions with ProductCD='W' and MCC 5968/5815/5817 (digital subscriptions) within 45-90 minutes
TransactionAmt clusters at $9.99, $14.99, $19.99, $24.99 with 80%+ of transactions in this range
card1_MID_count=1 for all merchants (first transaction with each subscription provider)
dist1 >800km on 90%+ of transactions indicating IP geolocation far from billing address
Transactions concentrated between txn_hour 2-5 with is_night=1 flag
P_emaildomain identical across all subscriptions but domain age <72 hours or disposable email pattern
card1_txn_count_24hr spikes from baseline <3 to >10 within single session
summary: A card suddenly registers 8-15 first-time subscription purchases ($10-$30 each) in under 90 minutes during overnight hours, all using the same newly-created email address and originating from an IP location 800+ km from the billing address.
reasoning: Criminals with stolen card details rush to extract maximum value before the legitimate cardholder notices, targeting low-friction subscription services they can quickly resell on dark web marketplaces before chargebacks arrive.
indicator_explanations: Card rapidly signs up for multiple streaming and digital subscription services
Transaction amounts match common subscription tiers across different providers
Every merchant in the burst is seeing this card for the first time
IP location is hundreds of miles away from the cardholder's billing address
All activity happens during overnight hours when cardholder likely sleeping
Same fresh or throwaway email used to register all the subscriptions
Card goes from normal low activity to frenzied burst in one session
